title: LLM Provider & Model Selection for the Explanation Layer
context: The explanation layer uses a configurable, provider-agnostic LLM. Task04 §3 mandates Claude Code agent integration for BUILDING the system (satisfied by the SDLC pipeline); the runtime explanation LLM is not vendor-pinned. Phase-1 uses Google Gemini (gemini-1.5-flash, free tier) to minimize pilot cost; Claude API (claude-3-5-haiku) is a supported alternate. All prompt templates are stored as versioned, auditable artifacts (not inline strings), meaning the model identifier is a field in the prompt artifact — model/provider upgrades require no source code changes, only a prompt artifact version bump. Per ADR-003, LLM calls are asynchronous and outside the 500ms SLA, so latency is not a primary selection criterion. The selection criteria are: (1) explanation quality — output must reliably contain one skill-alignment statement, one behavioral/work-style statement, and one growth/career statement per AC3; (2) cost at MVP scale (estimated 500–5,000 explanation calls/day during pilot); (3) compatibility with the versioned prompt template approach; (4) availability and rate-limit headroom on the LLM provider's API.

options_considered.name: Google Gemini (gemini-1.5-flash) — Phase-1 default
pros: Free tier available via Google AI Studio: zero cost at MVP pilot scale
Fast response time (~400–900ms for a 250-token explanation), reducing async queue drain time
Sufficient for structured template-driven output where the prompt tightly constrains the explanation format (three mandatory sentence types)
Easily upgradeable: model change requires only editing the prompt artifact JSON — no code change
cons: Free tier has rate limits that may require throttling under burst load
Requires GEMINI_API_KEY environment variable; developers must obtain a key from Google AI Studio

options_considered.name: claude-3-haiku-20240307 (claude-3-haiku)
pros: Lowest cost among Claude 3 models: ~$0.25/M input tokens, ~$1.25/M output tokens — approximately 20× cheaper than Opus
Fastest response time among Claude 3 models (~400–800ms for a 250-token explanation), reducing async queue drain time
Sufficient for structured template-driven output where the prompt tightly constrains the explanation format (three mandatory sentence types)
High rate limits on Anthropic API allow throughput scaling without quota concerns at MVP scale
Easily upgradeable: model change requires only editing the prompt artifact JSON — no code change
cons: Occasionally produces less nuanced or more formulaic explanations than larger models, particularly for edge-case profile combinations
May require more explicit prompt engineering to reliably produce the three mandatory sentence components on adversarial or ambiguous inputs

options_considered.name: claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022 (claude-3-5-sonnet)
pros: Significantly better reasoning and explanation quality vs Haiku, especially for nuanced behavioral alignment analysis
More robust against adversarial or incomplete profile inputs
Better at multi-step reasoning in a single prompt (skill + behavioral + growth in one coherent paragraph)
cons: ~3× more expensive than Haiku (~$3/M input, ~$15/M output)
Slower (~800–2000ms per explanation), increasing async queue processing time
Quality difference is partially offset by the structured prompt template that constrains output format regardless of model capability

options_considered.name: claude-3-opus-20240229 (claude-3-opus)
pros: Highest explanation quality; most sophisticated behavioral reasoning
Best at detecting subtle work-style incompatibilities and articulating growth potential
cons: ~15× more expensive than Haiku (~$15/M input, ~$75/M output); prohibitive for MVP pilot at any meaningful scale
Slowest model; async queue back-pressure under load
Explanation quality advantage is marginal for structured, template-constrained outputs — the prompt artifact already defines the required sections

decision: Use Google Gemini (gemini-1.5-flash) as the Phase-1 default for the explanation layer (free tier minimizes pilot cost). The model identifier is stored as a field in the versioned prompt artifact (artifacts/prompts/match_explanation_v1.json) under the key 'model_name'. Claude API (claude-3-5-haiku) is a supported alternate, selectable by changing only the prompt artifact's model_name field — no source change required. The prompt template is structured to enforce three mandatory explanation sections via numbered output format instructions, so any provider reliably produces the required skill-alignment, behavioral-alignment, and growth/career statements. A regression test in the QA suite validates that the explanation output always contains all three section types, alerting the team to prompt drift after any model or provider change.
consequences: Explanation quality at MVP scale is sufficient for pilot validation at zero LLM cost (Gemini free tier). To switch to Claude API (claude-3-5-haiku-20241022 for production hardening), a new prompt artifact version (match_explanation_v2.json) is published with only the model_name field changed; no Python source files are modified. Cost estimate for Claude API comparison: 5,000 explanations/day at ~300 tokens input + ~200 tokens output per call = ~$1.50/day (Haiku) vs ~$22.50/day (Sonnet). The prompt artifact must include explicit output format constraints (e.g., 'Section 1: Skill Alignment — [sentence]. Section 2: Behavioral Fit — [sentence]. Section 3: Growth Potential — [sentence].') to ensure reliable structured output regardless of provider. The explanation structure regression test (AC3, AC12) is mandatory — it acts as the early-warning system for model version drift or prompt degradation. If the regression test failure rate exceeds 5% in production monitoring, the migration playbook is to increment the prompt artifact to v2 with an alternative provider/model, requiring zero source code changes.
